title: EIP-8016: SSZ CompatibleUnion
description: Introduces a new SSZ composite type, CompatibleUnion, for representing tagged unions with forward-compatible Merkleization. Each field is assigned the same stable generalized index (gindex) across all type options, so Merkle proof verifiers can check common fields without tracking version-specific gindex maps. Serialization uses a one-byte selector prefix, and compatibility rules ensure all union variants share a consistent tree shape.
author: Etan Kissling (@etan-status), Cayman (@wemeetagain)
laymanDescription: When Ethereum has multiple versions of the same data structure (like different transaction types), verifiers currently need to know which version they are checking. CompatibleUnion is a new data format that keeps proof paths identical across all versions, so verifiers work without version-specific knowledge and do not break when new versions are added.

endUsers.description: No direct impact. End users benefit indirectly through more robust light clients and bridges that handle multi-variant data without version-specific breakage.

appDevs.description: Smart contracts verifying Merkle proofs across multiple data variants (e.g., transaction types) gain stable gindices, reducing proof logic complexity.

walletDevs.description: Minimal direct impact. Wallets relying on light client proofs benefit from consistent proof paths across data variants without wallet-side updates.

toolingInfra.description: SSZ libraries and proof tooling must implement CompatibleUnion with selector-based serialization, compatibility validation, and the mix_in_selector Merkleization extension.

layer2s.description: Bridges and L2s verifying Merkle proofs across multiple data variants benefit from stable gindices, avoiding verifier updates when new variants are introduced.

stakersNodes.description: No operational changes for stakers. Validators benefit from a cleaner upgrade path when new data variants are introduced across forks.

clClients.description: Consensus clients must implement the CompatibleUnion SSZ type, including selector-based serialization, compatibility checks, and mix_in_selector Merkleization.

elClients.description: Minimal direct impact. Execution clients may need SSZ library updates if they consume or produce data encoded with CompatibleUnion.

benefits: Guarantees stable generalized indices across union variants, preventing Merkle proof breakage.
Simplifies verifiers by removing the need for version-specific gindex maps.
Enables adding or removing union variants without breaking existing proof consumers.
Compact serialization with a single-byte selector prefix and no padding overhead.
tradeoffs: All union variants must share compatible Merkleization, constraining schema design.
SSZ libraries and tooling must implement the new type and compatibility validation logic.

improveUX.description: Stable Merkle proofs across data variants reduce maintenance for light client and bridge developers, simplifying cross-version verification.
